title: Simnel cake
subtitle: The Easter fruit cake with two layers of marzipan
excerpt: A light fruit cake with a layer of marzipan baked through the centre and a second layer pressed on top, studded with eleven marzipan balls for the apostles. Traditional for Easter and Mothering Sunday.
sourceNotes: Simnel cake is documented in British baking literature from at least the seventeenth century. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, gives a recipe for a rich fruit cake with almond paste baked through the centre and glazed on top, consistent with the modern version. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932), records the eleven apostle balls as a Victorian addition. The association with Easter and Mothering Sunday is well attested in English food history.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the top marzipan decoration. Thaw fully at room temperature before adding the toasted marzipan topping.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps well for a week in an airtight tin; the marzipan top is best added on the day or the day before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pliable paste made from ground almonds and sugar, used in baking for modelling, filling, and covering cakes. It softens and colours under high heat, caramelising if briefly grilled.

content.text: Simnel cake is the traditional British Easter and Mothering Sunday cake. Its distinction is structural: a layer of

is pressed into the middle of the batter before it goes into the oven, where it stays soft and yielding through the bake. A second round of marzipan is laid on top of the cooled cake and briefly grilled or torched to brown it, then decorated with eleven marzipan balls — one for each of the apostles, Judas excluded.

The cake itself is a lightly spiced fruit cake, less dense than a Christmas cake: currants, sultanas, and mixed peel in a creamed butter and egg sponge. It keeps well for a week in a tin, and the marzipan layers make it unusually moist. The eleven balls are rolled by hand; they take about five minutes and are the most visible part of the finished cake.

attrs.prepNote: divided: 150 g for the middle layer, 200 g for the top, 100 g for the eleven balls

content.text: Prepare the tin and cream the batter

Heat the oven to 150°C fan (170°C conventional). Double-line the base and sides of a 20 cm round cake tin with baking paper, as the long bake time means the outside can over-colour without protection. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{light-brown-sugar}} together until pale and fluffy, about four minutes. Add {{eggs}} one at a time, beating well between each. Fold in {{plain-flour}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{lemon}} zest, {{currants}}, and {{sultanas}}.

Roll the 150 g portion of {{marzipan}} to a circle the diameter of the tin. Spoon half the batter into the tin and level it. Lay the marzipan circle on top and press it gently flat. Spoon the remaining batter over it and level the surface.

Bake for 85–90 minutes until the surface is deep brown and a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean with no wet batter. Test in two places to avoid hitting the marzipan layer. An oven thermometer matters here; low heat means an under-baked centre while the top over-colours. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then on a rack completely.

Warm the {{apricot-jam}} briefly and brush it over the top of the cooled cake. Roll the 200 g portion of {{marzipan}} to a circle the size of the top and press it on. Roll the remaining 100 g of {{marzipan}} into eleven equal balls and arrange them in a ring around the edge of the marzipan top, pressing gently to adhere. Brown the top with a kitchen torch or under a hot grill for 1–2 minutes, watching constantly.
The balls must be uniform in size for a tidy finish. Divide the 100 g piece into eleven equal portions by weight and roll each one between your palms.
The cake is also traditional for Mothering Sunday, the fourth Sunday of Lent (roughly three weeks before Easter).
If the batter curdles when you add the eggs, add a tablespoon of flour from the recipe with the last egg.
